observations: Ground-mounted commercial or rural solar array visible — multiple rows of panels on metal racking across an open paddock.
Worker appears to be pitching to a customer, likely using this or a similar site as a reference installation.
No installer currently on tools — this is a customer pitch or site walk scenario.
Sky is partly cloudy — good conditions to discuss real-world generation variability with the customer.

salesPitchFeedback.message: Opening line 'Uh hi, yeah we're doing solar in the area' is weak — the filler 'uh' and vague framing kills first impressions. Lead with your name, your company, and a specific local credibility hook. Try: 'G'day, I'm [Name] from [Company]. We just finished a system two streets over and I wanted to let your neighbours know while we're here.'

'It's pretty good, you'd save some money I reckon' is too vague and lacks confidence. Customers need a specific, believable number to act. Try: 'A typical home like yours in this area saves around $1,800 to $2,400 a year on power bills — most of our customers are in profit within 4 to 5 years.'

Closing with 'Do you want to maybe think about it?' is the weakest possible close — it invites a 'no' and hands control to the customer. Replace with an assumptive or choice close. Try: 'I can book a free site assessment for Tuesday or Thursday this week — which suits you better?'

No mention of warranty, battery storage, feed-in tariffs, or government rebates (STC/STCs). These are powerful trust and urgency builders in the Australian market. Weave them in: 'Our panels carry a 25-year performance warranty and you're still eligible for government rebates right now — that won't last forever.'

Rapport was not established before jumping to the pitch. Start with a genuine observation or question: 'Have you had a look at what your power bills have been doing lately?' — this gets the customer talking and frames your solution as a response to their pain, not a cold sell.

timeOnTaskNote: Pitch appears to have moved too quickly past the rapport-building phase and stalled at a soft close. A well-structured door-knock pitch should run 3 to 5 minutes — spend the first 60 seconds on rapport and the customer's current bill pain before quoting savings.
nextSteps: Reopen the conversation with a bill-pain question: 'Have your power bills gone up much in the last 12 months?' — get the customer engaged before talking savings.
Anchor the value with a specific payback figure relevant to their property size and typical usage, referencing the local reference site if visible.
Close with a choice of two appointment times, not an open-ended 'think about it' — book the site assessment before you leave the door.

visualCallouts.label: Reference site upsell
visualCallouts.message: Point to this array — use it as social proof. Tell the customer: this is what a completed system looks like, and your neighbours are already saving.

visualCallouts.label: Generation story
visualCallouts.message: Partly cloudy sky — use this to address the objection 'does it work on cloudy days?' Modern panels still produce 25 to 40 percent on overcast days.

spokenCue.say: Reopen with a bill question before you pitch savings.
